articles.title: THE HIDDEN STACK BEHIND AI APPS THAT MAKE IT TO PRODUCTION
articles.summary: Most AI apps fail to reach production not because of models, but because of the invisible infrastructure layer underneath. Enterprise customers expect identity that works with their provider, permissions that reflect their org, directory sync that provisions their users, and security that responds in real time.

articles.title: Learn how leading AI teams do it →
THREE DOMINANT MODELS OF TODAY'S VERTICAL AI HORSERACE
articles.summary: Public SaaS market caps continue to free-fall while VC keeps investing in vertical AI at frothy valuations. There are three models emerging, and they require completely different playbooks. Model 1 sells AI-powered software to incumbents - de-risked GTM, but innovation ceiling. Model 2 acquires businesses and deploys AI to improve unit economics across a portfolio - immediate revenue, but integration nightmares. Model 3 builds the business from scratch as an AI-native replacement - maximum value capture, but cold start and regulatory hurdles. Many companies are choosing a model whose physics don't match their market.

articles.title: YOU'VE BEEN KICKED OUT OF THE ARENA, YOU JUST DON'T KNOW IT YET
articles.summary: Humans can't compete with AI anymore, and most won't notice until it's too late. Companies are now competing with startups that can do everything they can do, but with AI, and without thinking about or working too hard. To compete, companies need to get everyone up-skilled immediately, start trying new tools, and remove barriers and the employees who are resistant to change. They should try to rebuild their product from scratch - if it takes less than two weeks, ask what that means for their company.

articles.title: BUILDING ON SAND: THE HIDDEN COST OF AI MODEL CHURN
articles.summary: AI pushes technical complexity upstream into the problem space, where the PM lives. This means the PM must become the architect because architecture is now a product artifact. An AI model update can invalidate assumptions, making it impossible to define products, user experience, feasibility, or cost. The only way to build durable AI products is to design systems that assume the model will change.

articles.title: HOW TO SELL TO AGENTS
articles.summary: In 1937, Ronald Coase asked why firms existed if markets are so efficient. His answer was transaction costs. AI agents are about to collapse those costs to near zero. An agent can discover a service, check its price, and call it in a single HTTP round-trip. HTTP 402 - "Payment Required" - has been reserved for future use since 1997. We're finally finding that use. The web was built for humans to browse. The next layer will be built for agents to buy.

articles.title: THE FUTURE OF B2B MARKETING IS AI AGENT RECOMMENDATIONS. AND AI AGENTS PLAY FAVORITES
articles.summary: 87% of B2B software buyers say AI chatbots are changing how they research. LLM traffic is projected to overtake traditional search by the end of 2026. Economic party is expected even sooner due to significantly higher conversion rates from AI-referred traffic. The companies that figure out how to become the recommended answer will have an almost insurmountable distribution advantage.

articles.title: WIN AI SEARCH VISIBILITY BEFORE YOUR COMPETITORS.
articles.summary: Search has shifted from links to AI-generated answers. This playbook breaks down what's actually working across 15M+ queries and how teams like Ramp, Carta, and Webflow [12] build systems that earn citations, increase share of voice, and turn AI visibility into measurable pipeline.

articles.title: CANARY
articles.summary: Canary is an AI QA engineer that reads your codebase.

articles.title: SAMORA
articles.summary: Multilingual voice agents for customer ops.

articles.title: FIGR AI
articles.summary: Figr is an AI product design agent for UX analysis and prototyping.

articles.title: NAVIGATING A PARADIGM SHIFT
articles.summary: The hardest part of a paradigm shift is navigating it while it's incomplete. Paradigm shifts are tectonic shifts beneath markets, technologies, and human thought. They begin quietly at first, then the world suddenly bends. Paradigm shifts begin with dissonance, not consensus, as the old ways no longer fit the new reality. The best founders understand why the current system works, but believe there are better ways to do things.

articles.title: A PRIMER ON FACTORY ECONOMICS FOR STARTUPS
articles.summary: Tesla popularized the "factory-is-the-product" thesis. It now applies to companies making drone motors, robot actuators, PCBs, and battery packs. The learning curve is central to strategy: for each doubling of cumulative production, costs fall predictably. Your competitive position depends on where you are on the curve, how fast you descend, and whether you can finance the journey.

articles.title: THE DARK SIDE OF BUILDING IN PUBLIC
articles.summary: Building in public means the criticism is public too.

articles.title: ZERO TO ONE IS A SUBTRACTION PROBLEM
articles.summary: Zero to one involves strategic choices about what features and design to prioritize, impacting a startup's early growth and learning.

articles.title: HIGH-FREQUENCY SOFTWARE
articles.summary: Software engineering will turn to focus on coding the 'machine that builds the machine' over the next five to ten years.
